perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall not disclose any Conf idential Information to any third parties for three (3) years following the date of its disclo sure by Disclosing Part y to Receiv ing Party, except to Receiving Party's consultants as set out below. However, Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information in accordance with judicial or other governmental order, provided that the Receiving Part y shall give the Disclosing Part y reasonable notice prior to such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall not disclose any Conf idential Information to any third parties for three (3) years following the date of its disclo sure by Disclosing Part y to Receiv ing Party, except to Receiving Party's consultants as set out below. However, Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information in accordance with judicial or other governmental order, provided that the Receiving Part y shall give the Disclosing Part y notice immediately before such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable notice' to 'notice immediately before such disclosure' makes it virtually impossible to comply with the notification requirement, potentially prejudicing the disclosing party's ability to seek protective orders. This is particularly problematic in jurisdictions where prior notice is crucial for preventing improper disclosure under legal proceedings.
perturbation.law_explanation: CPR 39.2 emphasizes the court's control over evidence. Providing notice 'immediately before' disclosure could hinder the disclosing party from effectively seeking a protective order from the court to control the use of confidential information presented as evidence, thereby undermining the court's ability to manage evidence fairly and protect confidential information as intended by the rules.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: collateral estoppel | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you collateral estoppel Collateral estoppel is an important doctrine in the fields of criminal law and civil procedure . In criminal law, collateral estoppel protects criminal defendants from being tried for the same issue in more than one criminal trial through the double jeopardy clause of the Fifth Amendment . As established in Benton v. Maryland , collateral estoppel is binding on both federal and state governments through incorporation by way of the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment . In civil procedure, collateral estoppel refers to the application of res judicata principles through issue preclusion . For issue preclusion, a party can utilize collateral estoppel to prevent another party from re-litigating any issue that has been validly, finally, and actually determined on the merits in a previous case. In deciding if any given issue has already been determined, the court will consider whether that issue was essential to the previous holding . Under this test, any issue previously raised in court which would change the outcome of the case had it been decided differently is deemed actually determined. Validity and finality for the purposes of collateral estoppel are determined by whether the first case would survive a collateral attack . Common reasons why a case may not survive a collateral attack include a lack of personal jurisdiction , a lack of subject matter jurisdiction , and a failure of due process in the first case. There are two forms of issue preclusion/collateral estoppel - offensive and defensive: Defensive issue preclusion occurs when the party being sued raises collateral estoppel. As established in Bernhard v. Bank of America , defensive issue preclusion does not require mutuality. In other words, a person who was not a party to the initial case can raise issue preclusion. For example, if a court determines that Frank cannot recover in a lawsuit against Sally because Frank was negligent , then Susan can raise collateral estoppel as to Frank’s negligence if she too is sued by Frank. Offensive issue preclusion occurs when the party who initiates the lawsuit/claim raises collateral estoppel against the defendant in a previous case. Offensive issue preclusion generally does require mutuality; meaning it can only be used by parties to the initial lawsuit, though a few exceptions exist. For example, if a court determines that Frank can recover against Sally because Sally was negligent, Susan generally cannot raise collateral estoppel as to Frank’s negligence in a second case against Frank. Courts have broad discretion as to whether they will allow non-mutual offensive issue preclusion, with a few common justifications outlined in Parklane Hosiery Co. v. Shore . These justifications include misaligned incentives in the first case, different procedural factors in the first case than in the second case, and whether the plaintiff in the second case could have easily joined the first case. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS the Constitution THE LEGAL PROCESS appellate procedure criminal law criminal procedure wex definitions civil procedure courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable notice' to 'notice immediately before' directly contradicts the ability of the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While the provided snippets do not directly address notice periods for protective orders, the Perturbation and Law Explanations clearly indicate that such a change would hinder the Disclosing Party's ability to effectively seek a protective order, rendering the notification requirement practically useless.

perturbation.original_text: If either party employs lawyers to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable lawyers fees. This Agreement shall be construed under the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: If either party employs lawyers to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover lawyers fees at a rate not to exceed 50 pounds per hour. This Agreement shall be construed under the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the lawyer's fees to 50 pounds per hour is highly unreasonable in England and Wales given the market rate of solicitors. This change is designed to deter the prevailing party from claiming reasonable lawyer's fees.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this SI deals with non-contentious business, it establishes principles of fairness and reasonableness in solicitors' fees, including factors like the complexity, skill, and time involved. A cap of £50/hour is unlikely to be considered reasonable, particularly in complex commercial disputes, conflicting with the intent of fair compensation principles underlying legal remuneration in England and Wales.

perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for the recovery of "reasonable lawyers fees," while the changed text imposes a cap of 50 pounds per hour. The Perturbation Explanation states that this rate is highly unreasonable in England and Wales, designed to deter the prevailing party from claiming reasonable lawyer's fees. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that a cap of £50/hour is unlikely to be considered reasonable, particularly in complex commercial disputes, which conflicts with the intent of fair compensation. Since the changed text introduces a cap on fees that the Perturbation Explanation says is unreasonable, which the Law Explanation appears to agree with, it contradicts the original text in a way that likely wouldn't hold up in court because it would be seen as bad faith.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own Confidential Information, to keep confidential the Confidential Information. Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information or Confidential Materials only to Receiving Part y's employees or bona-fidae consultants on a need-to-know basis. Receiving Party will have executed or sh all execute appropriate written agreements with its employees and consultants sufficient to enable them to comp ly with all the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall take security precautions equivalent to using a password 'password123' to keep confidential the Confidential Information. Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information or Confidential Materials only to Receiving Part y's employees or bona-fidae consultants on a need-to-know basis. Receiving Party will have executed or sh all execute appropriate written agreements with its employees and consultants sufficient to enable them to comp ly with all the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Suggesting that 'password123' is a reasonable security precaution undermines the agreement by effectively setting an incredibly low and easily breached security standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of the GDPR requires appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including pseudonymisation and encryption. Using a simple and easily guessable password like 'password123' as the *only* measure is a blatant violation of this requirement, as it does not provide an appropriate level of security for confidential information, especially in a business context. It means that the receiving party is not implementing 'appropriate technical and organizational measures'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 32 GDPR – Security of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. Adherence to an approved code of conduct as referred to in Article 40 or an approved certification mechanism as referred to in Article 42 may be used as an element by which to demonstrate compliance with the requirements set out in paragraph 1 of this Article. The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law. Suitable Recitals ( 75 ) Risks to the Rights and Freedoms of Natural Person
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by suggesting that 'password123' is a reasonable security precaution, which directly contradicts Article 32 of the GDPR. The original text mandated 'reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own Confidential Information.' By replacing this with a specific, weak password suggestion, the changed text fails to implement 'appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk,' as required by the GDPR. The scraped snippet explicitly states the need to implement 'appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data.' The use of such a weak password does not align with the security level required by GDPR, therefore a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorised disclosure of Confidential Informat ion and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies , to such compensation as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorised disclosure of Confidential Informat ion and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies , to such compensation as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction, but not to exceed £100.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause that sets a very low upper limit (£100) on the damages recoverable defeats the purpose of stating that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy and contradicts the spirit of seeking proper compensation as deemed by a court.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law, principles of 'fair compensation' for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, Hadley v Baxendale is a foundational case establishing the principles of remoteness of damage in contract law. It dictates that damages should fairly compensate the injured party for losses arising naturally from the breach or those reasonably contemplated by both parties at the time of the contract. Limiting damages to £100 is unlikely to provide fair compensation in many scenarios involving breaches of confidentiality, particularly those involving substantial commercial harm, and goes against the reasonable contemplation principle in Hadley v Baxendale.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Hadley v Baxendale — e-lawresources.co.uk 0 Hadley v Baxendale [1854] EWHC Exch J70 Courts of Exchequer The crankshaft broke in the Claimant’s mill. He engaged the services of the Defendant to deliver the crankshaft to the place where it was to be repaired and to subsequently return it after it had been repaired. Due to neglect of the Defendant, the crankshaft was returned 7 days late. The Claimant was unable to use the mill during this time and claimed for loss of profit. The Defendant argued that he was unaware that the mill would have to be closed during the delay and therefore the loss of profit was too remote. Held: The damages available for breach of contract include: 1. Those which may fairly and reasonably be considered arising naturally from the breach of contract or 2. Such damages as may reasonably be supposed to have been in the contemplation of both the parties at the time the contract was made. If any special circumstances exists which were actually communicated to the Defendant, the Claimant may recover any damages which would ordinarily follow from a breach of contract under the special circumstances communicated. Back to lecture outline on remedies in contract law
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the principle established in Hadley v Baxendale, which dictates that damages should fairly compensate the injured party. The added clause limiting damages to £100 directly undermines the possibility of fair compensation, particularly in cases involving significant confidential information breaches, as described in the Law Explanation. The scraped law snippet from e-lawresources.co.uk about Hadley v Baxendale highlights that damages should arise naturally from the breach or be reasonably contemplated by both parties. A £100 limit likely fails to meet this standard in many scenarios, thus creating a legal contradiction.
